coredata.dc:description: Since ceramic open-cell foams are mostly utilized in high-temperature applications, investigation of their thermophysical properties, regarding radiative heat transfer, is crucial. This study comprises a comprehensive characterization of the radiative properties of various ceramic foams, considering their specific scattering behavior. Spectroscopic measurements performed with a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer are combined with a numerical parameter identification procedure to establish extinction coefficients and scattering albedos using appropriate scattering phase functions. Fundamental differences in the radiation behavior of the foams are demonstrated, depending on whether the struts behave as semi-transparent or opaque. A parametric study revealed the influence of several material and structural parameters on the spectral or temperature-dependent extinction coefficients. At room temperature, when all ceramics behave opaquely, extinction coefficients are affected by the surface reflectivity, but mainly by the geometric properties of the foams (porosity, pore size). In contrast to carbon-containing foams with nearly constant radiative properties, extinction coefficients of foams made of oxidic ceramics show a nearly linear increase with increasing temperature. Validation is achieved by comparing model predictions, using Rosseland diffusion approximation and identified radiative properties, with measurement results at up to 700 °C of the effective thermal conductivity obtained from the transient plane source method (Hot Disk). Deviations largely amount to ±10 % for a pure alumina, as well as differently coated foams, when considering appropriate sample thickness and anisotropic scattering. Besides confirming the reasonability of the identified radiative properties, the suitability of both measurement devices and the simplified modelling procedure for ceramic open-cell foams is thus demonstrated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since ceramic open-cell foams are mostly utilized in high-temperature applications, investigation of their thermophysical properties, regarding radiative heat transfer, is crucial. This study comprises a comprehensive characterization of the radiative properties of various ceramic foams, considering their specific scattering behavior. Spectroscopic measurements performed with a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer are combined with a numerical parameter identification procedure to establish extinction coefficients and scattering albedos using appropriate scattering phase functions. Fundamental differences in the radiation behavior of the foams are demonstrated, depending on whether the struts behave as semi-transparent or opaque. A parametric study revealed the influence of several material and structural parameters on the spectral or temperature-dependent extinction coefficients. At room temperature, when all ceramics behave opaquely, extinction coefficients are affected by the surface reflectivity, but mainly by the geometric properties of the foams (porosity, pore size). In contrast to carbon-containing foams with nearly constant radiative properties, extinction coefficients of foams made of oxidic ceramics show a nearly linear increase with increasing temperature. Validation is achieved by comparing model predictions, using Rosseland diffusion approximation and identified radiative properties, with measurement results at up to 700 °C of the effective thermal conductivity obtained from the transient plane source method (Hot Disk). Deviations largely amount to ±10 % for a pure alumina, as well as differently coated foams, when considering appropriate sample thickness and anisotropic scattering. Besides confirming the reasonability of the identified radiative properties, the suitability of both measurement devices and the simplified modelling procedure for ceramic open-cell foams is thus demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Ceramic open-cell foams are encountered in a wide range of applications, as they can be used in numerous branches of industry due to their many advantageous properties, such as their high temperature resistance, low density, large specific surface area and beneficial flow properties [1]. Besides serving as filters for metal melts, they are also applied as solar receivers for solar thermal power plants, in chemical reactors as catalyst support, in porous burners and in heat exchangers [1]. Since these applications mostly involve high temperatures, it is essential to consider radiative heat transfer during the design and optimization process of the systems.

Commonly, radiation in porous media is modelled by the homogenization approach, describing the two phases as one homogeneous, semi-transparent medium with volumetric, effective properties [2–4]. According to Baillis et al. [2], this approach can be applied for porous media with a system size significantly larger than the investigated wavelength range and when the medium dimensions are sufficiently large compared to the particle or cell diameter. The corresponding transport problem is then governed by the radiative transfer equation (RTE). As mentioned by Cunsolo et al. [3], the homogeneous medium approach is sufficiently accurate as long as the relevant radiative properties, namely the extinction coefficient (absorption and scattering coefficient), the scattering albedo and the scattering phase function, are accurately specified.

Due to the complex, irregular geometry of open-cell foams, the determination of their radiative properties poses a particular challenge. Simplified methods like empirical or analytical correlations [3,5–11] or the tomographic image technique [12] allow a quick estimation of the radiative properties of the materials, in particular the extinction coefficient, but might suffer from low accuracy and limited applicability. To reproduce the radiative behavior of the material more accurately, the MCRT (Monte Carlo Ray Tracing) technique [3,7,8,13–17] can be applied, considering the exact porous structure digitized from computed tomography (CT) scans or from an idealized model structure. The method offers the possibility to compare radiative properties across different scales [18]. However, the MCRT method can be computationally expensive and time-consuming as well as error-prone, since it requires the knowledge of the wavelength-dependent radiative behavior of the strut material as well as exact structural parameters, including strut porosity [19].

Despite the requirement for special measurement, experimental examination of the radiative behavior of the foams probably leads to the most reliable results. Experiments can be either carried out by spectroscopic measurements [5,6,9,11,20–22], typically performed using a Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer or other thermal measurements [10,23–25]. To derive radiative properties from the measured radiative behavior of the foam (e.g., hemispherical or directional transmittance or reflectance), an appropriate evaluation procedure is required. In the simplest case, Bouguer’s Law, often also referred to as Lambert-Beer’s Law, is applied to estimate the extinction coefficients from transmittance data [5,6,11,22,26]. However, it has to be considered that this simplification only includes the attenuation by absorption and scattering by the medium and may introduce errors for materials with a considerable, mainly forward-directed scattering [26]. In contrast, an inverse analysis or parameter identification method enables the consideration of the characteristic scattering behavior of the material. This allows the deduction of several radiative parameters from hemispherical transmittance and reflectance measurements by inversely solving the full RTE, for example, using the discrete ordinate method [9,20,21]. This approach seems to be suited for foams made of oxidic ceramics with wavelength-dependent, partially semi-transparent radiation behavior, dominated by surface and volume scattering [27] at certain wavelengths.

Using the methods described above, numerous authors determined the radiative properties of ceramic open-cell foams made of opaque [3,8,15–17,20–22], semi-transparent [7,11,13,14,23–25] or both types [5,6,9] of ceramic materials, in some cases involving a large variation of parameters like porosity or pore size [6–8,11,13,15,16]. However, experimental determination of the radiative properties of open-cell foams made from various ceramic materials, including semi-transparent ceramics, while explicitly considering their specific scattering behavior, has not yet been comprehensively investigated and needs further research. Given the possible application temperatures of the ceramics, investigations should cover a wide wavelength range, especially shorter wavelengths relevant for high temperatures.

In the present study, spectroscopic measurements in the near (NIR) and mid (MIR) infrared were performed using an FTIR spectrometer to obtain the spectral directional-hemispherical transmittance as well as reflectance of various ceramic open-cell foams in the wavelength range of 0.9 – 16 μm. These data served as input for a numerical parameter identification procedure to evaluate the extinction coefficient and scattering albedo of the samples. An important aspect of the study is the scattering phase function, i.e., the distribution of incoming radiation scattered by the foam struts: A short literature review discussing the suitability of different scattering phase functions for specific foam types is provided and the influence of the scattering phase function on the identified radiative properties is demonstrated. Subsequently, several parametric investigations concerning the effect of sample thickness, pore size, material and coating on the extinction coefficients of the foams are presented. Finally, a validation is performed by comparing model predictions with measurements of the effective thermal conductivity at temperatures up to 700 °C.

For the present study, different laboratory-produced and commercial ceramic open-cell foams prepared by replica techniques were considered. The foams were mainly made of semi-transparent strut materials (pure (p) and composite (c) alumina (Al2O3), zirconia (ZrO2)) or of opaque materials (silicon carbide (SiC) and carbon-bonded alumina (Al2O3-C)). As part of a further parametric study, the effects of the linear pore density in the range of 10–30 pores per inch (ppi), and the foam thickness on the radiative properties were analyzed. Furthermore, the influence of different coatings (spinel (MgAl2O4), titanium dioxide (TiO2), carbon-bonded alumina (Al2O3-C)), applied on Al2O3 (p) foams, was examined. Table 1 lists all the investigated foams with their respective properties.

The approximate material composition was either provided by the manufacturer or measured by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements. The total porosity ψ of most foams, comprising both the open porosity of the foams themselves and the internal porosity of the struts, was determined from CT scans (CT-ALPHA system by ProCon X-Ray GmbH Garbsen, resolution of 39 μm) using the imaging processing software ImageJ. The advantage of this method is that the variation of the porosity along the foam depth can be obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , foams of smaller pore sizes (Al2O3 (c) 30 ppi) can feature a significant porosity change across the sample thickness with an increased porosity in the center of the sample and a reduced porosity near the edges, which result from the manufacturing process.

For foams whose geometry was not available from 3D CT scans, the average porosities were estimated from their raw density. Pore sizes were measured manually with a digital caliper in all three directions and subsequently averaged. The standard size of the samples for spectroscopic measurements was approx. 50 mm x 50 mm x 20 mm, while thermal conductivity measurements were performed on larger samples (90 mm x 90 mm x 30 mm) of the same foam types with comparable pore sizes and porosities. In all cases, the investigations were carried out in the direction of the short side, i.e., along the depth of the foam. Exact sample thicknesses necessary for the calculation of the radiative parameters were measured with a digital caliper. Approximate coating thicknesses determined by optical microscopic measurements are provided in Table 1. Further estimated geometric characteristics of the Al2O3 (p) 10 ppi foam necessary for the application of the empirical correlation by Li et al. [7] are summarized in Table 2 . Additionally, spectroscopic measurements on the base material Al2O3 (p) were performed on sintered disks of approx. 20 mm diameter and 1 mm thickness.

For the spectroscopic measurements, a Bruker Vertex 80v FTIR spectrometer was equipped with an external, gold-coated integrating sphere with an inner diameter of 200 mm (see Fig. 2 ). This setup enabled the measurement of the directional-hemispherical transmittance (TR) and reflectance (REFL) and delivers representative results even for foams with low ppi number, since the beam diameter reaches approx. 25 mm (sample diameter min. 50 mm). Equivalently, an internal integrating sphere was used for smaller, more homogeneous samples (beam diameter approx. 8 mm, sample diameter min. 20 mm), such as for the determination of the radiative properties of the struts performed on thin ceramic disks made of the same material.

The results presented in the following sections were obtained using two measurement setups: In the NIR (wavelength λ: 0.9 µm −2.4 µm), a CaF2 beam splitter and an indium gallium arsenide (InGaAs) detector were used and, in the MIR (wavelength λ: 2.0 µm −16 µm), a KBr beam splitter and a mercury-cadmium-telluride (MCT) detector were combined. The two measurement ranges were merged at λ = 2.3 µm. At this wavelength, slight offsets in the measurement data may occur due to the change in the optical components and the remounting of the samples.

For each foam with a thickness of s ≈ 20 mm listed in Table 1, measurements on both sides of three samples were conducted and their spectral average was taken. For foams whose sample thickness has been reduced by sawing or grinding (s ≈ 5–15 mm), a single measurement was performed on six samples with the beam impacting on the unmodified sample side, resulting in an average spectrum of six individual measurements. To reduce noise, each measurement represents the average of ten single spectra acquired on the same sample, always with a previously measured reference spectrum. The spectra were recorded with 32 scans at a wavenumber resolution of 4 cm−1. For the presentation and evaluation of the radiative parameters, data were processed by spectral averaging over wavelength intervals of Δλ = 0.1 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiative transfer equation and radiative properties
ce:section.ce:para:
A common approach is to treat the porous structure as a homogeneous medium with equivalent properties, allowing the application of the RTE to calculate the radiative heat transfer. For this, the attenuation of radiation is assumed to follow the exponential extinction law, as proved, for example, by Taine and Enguehard [28]. Under the assumption of azimuthal symmetry, the 1D-RTE for a homogeneous, scattering and absorbing medium at every wavelength is commonly given by Eq. (1). Thereby I is the radiative intensity, which is scattered from an incident direction µ’ (directing cosine of polar angle) to the direction µ (directing cosine of polar angle) and z is the spatial coordinate through the sample [25,29–32]. Since measurements are performed at room temperature, the sample is considered as a cold medium and self-emission by the material is negligible [30,33].

The relevant radiative properties are the extinction coefficient β , the scattering coefficient σ s and the scattering phase function

The extinction coefficient β represents the attenuation rate of the radiation passing through the material, independent of the sample thickness, and stands for the inverse of the mean free path of the radiation [25,26,29,30]. It is composed of the scattering coefficient σ s and the absorption coefficient α. A further important physical quantity is the optical thickness
τ = β · s
, which relates the extinction coefficient with the sample thickness s [26,29,30]. The scattering albedo
ω =
σ s
/ β
is described by the fraction of the scattering within the total extinction and is frequently used in the context of radiative parameters instead of the absorption/scattering coefficient [25,29,30,32].

The scattering phase function represents the probability of the directions into which incoming radiation from a specific direction will be scattered and therefore represents the distribution of scattered energy [25,26,29,30,32]. Since the scattering phase function in Eq. (1)

The parameter identification method allows the consideration of the complete scattering behavior of radiation by the material, including the increase of the radiation intensity due to forward scattering. This approach is generally based on minimizing the difference between experimentally and numerically obtained radiative intensities to determine the radiative properties of the sample [9,20,21,34]. In the present study, a numerical procedure employing the simulation of the radiative heat transfer was used to evaluate the extinction coefficient and scattering albedo required to match the experimentally determined values of directional-hemispherical transmittance and reflectance. The evaluation was carried out by minimization using the Nelder-Mead algorithm [35]. The radiative heat transfer was simulated by numerical solution of the dimensionless 1D steady-state RTE for a plane-parallel gray porous medium using an in-house code employing the discrete transfer method [30,36]. The code was validated by comparison with experimental data and identified radiative properties presented by Kaemmerlen et al. [37], Tilioua et al. [38] and Celzard et al. [39].

Since thermal radiation is the dominant mode of heat transfer, conduction and convection were neglected. The incident radiation was assumed to enter as a parallel beam. For discretization, the porous medium was divided into 300 segments, and 64 rays were utilized to represent polar angles from 0° to 180° The scattering phase function was discretized by 24 azimuthal angles. For these parameters, the difference in reflectivity and transmissivity compared to a solution obtained with twice the resolution was <0.2 %, indicating discretization-independent results. The convergence of the solution was ascertained by monitoring the maximum change in the solution between two iterations. Since the code had to be executed repeatedly during the minimization process, a shared memory parallelization was adopted to reduce computation time.

As the parameter identification is used to determine the extinction coefficient and scattering albedo from the two measured variables, the scattering phase function of the sample must be specified in advance. For isotropic scattering (ISO, see Fig. 3 (i)), the scattering phase function is
Φ

For open-cell ceramic foams, distinguishing between opaque and semi-transparent ceramic strut materials proved to be essential. For foams with opaque struts, most studies found the strongly backward-orientated scattering phase function of large opaque diffusely reflecting spheres (DRS, see Fig. 3(i), Eq. (3)) is very suitable [3,15–17,40]. The associated asymmetry factor is g = −0.44 (calculated by Eq. (2) applied to Eq. (3)).

In the present investigation, this phase function should be applicable for carbon-containing foams like SiC or Al2O3-C (coated) foams and for oxidic ceramic foams at wavelengths where the oxides behave as opaque for the infrared radiation. Furthermore, it was found that the DRS phase function for foams with opaque strut material is independent of structural parameters and the surface reflectance [16]. This means that it can theoretically be applied to such foams of all pore sizes, porosities and materials.

A distinct property of oxide ceramics, such as Al2O3 or ZrO2, is their wavelength-dependent radiation behavior and their semi-transparency for the short-wavelength IR (λ < 7 μm) [7,13,41]. Therefore, the scattering phase function of these ceramic foams is likewise wavelength-dependent and considerably complex. Studies have demonstrated that the scattering behavior and asymmetry factor are closely related to the geometric and optical properties of the struts [7,13]. With low optical thickness and scattering albedo of the strut material, radiation will be preferably scattered in the forward direction. In contrast, an increasing optical thickness of the strut causes backward scattering of the radiation [7]. Li et al. [14] suggested scattering distributions for an alumina foam (s = 20 mm, d p = 3.15 mm, ψ = 0.88), showing a transition from backward scattering to strongly predominant forward scattering with increasing wavelength from 0.4 μm to 7 μm due to increasing transparency of the ceramic material (see Fig. 3(ii)). In addition, Li et al. [7] proposed a correlation (Eq. (4)) with which the asymmetry factor g can be predicted based on structural parameters (t, k, see Table 2) and solid phase properties (scattering albedo ω 0, optical thickness τ 0, refractive index n 0).

A comparison of the measured and modelled effective thermal conductivity (ETC) serves to validate the modelling procedure and confirm the reasonability of the identified radiative parameters. Measurements up to 700 °C were performed using the transient plane source method (Hot Disk Method, sensor: M4922L, sensor diameter approx. 29 mm) combined with a muffle furnace flushed with nitrogen. A general measurement uncertainty of ± 5 % is given by the manufacturer. The method and further measurement conditions are presented in [42] and not explained here in detail for brevity.

The ETC can be modelled with reasonable accuracy by superimposing conductive and radiative heat transfer

[4,43]. Convective heat transfer is usually negligible, since pores are sufficiently small to suppress natural convection.

The thermal conductivity of the foam structure k cond is estimated from a resistance model for a cubic unit cell (Eq. (5)), considering the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity of the base material and the nitrogen atmosphere [44]. The relative strut thickness b is calculated from the total porosity ψ.

This procedure is only applicable to foams with pure bulk materials like Al2O3 (p) [45] with known solid thermal conductivity, not to foams with heterogeneous material composition. In addition, the resulting conductive thermal conductivity of Al2O3(p) foams with different coatings can be considered based on a resistance model using series and parallel connections and the respective thermal conductivities and thicknesses of Al2O3 struts and the coating material (MgAl2O4, TiO2, Al2O3-C) [46].

The radiative conductivity k rad can be determined using the Rosseland diffusion approximation, which is valid for optically thick, isotropically scattering and absorbing media when radiation travels only a short distance before being fully scattered or absorbed [6,11,22,26,37,43]. However, there is no strict definition regarding the optical thickness at which a material can be considered as optically thick. According to the literature, a medium can be assumed to be optically thick when τ >> 1 [30], or, according to [44], when τ > 15 at all relevant wavelenght.. Siegel and Howell [26] stated that the diffusion approach can be applied for τ > 5, but this value depends on the geometry and the conditions of the respective transport problem. Mendes et al. [4] obtained the ETC of two different metal foams using a coupled radiation-conduction model considering the radiative heat flux by solving the 1D RTE and from the superposition of conduction and radiation calculated by Rosseland approximation and compared it with measurement data. A limit of τ ≥ 10 is mentioned for the application of the Rosseland approximation [4]. While the solution of the RTE leads to very accurate results, the Rosseland approximation also allows estimation of heat transfer and ETC with good accuracy, with a maximum deviation of the ETC at 800 °C of approx. + 6 % at τ ≈ 8 and + 12 % at τ ≈ 4 [4]. For the present investigation, the optical thickness of an Al2O3 (p) foam amounts to τ > 8 or even τ > 10 for higher temperatures (calculated using results from Section 3), suggesting that the Rosseland approximation is applicable with acceptable accuracy.

Within the Rosseland approximation, the radiative heat transfer is treated as a diffusion process and the radiative conductivity k rad at different temperatures can be calculated by Eq. (6), including the Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ, the refractive index n (approx. 1 for high porosity foams) and the Rosseland mean extinction coefficient
β R
[6,11,22,26,30,37,43]. With an increasing extinction coefficient
β R
, the radiative conductivity is reduced, as the attenuation of the radiation hinders radiative heat transfer.

This coefficient
β R
can be compared with an integrated, spectrally weighted extinction coefficient according to Planck’s radiation spectrum at the respective temperature (see Fig. 4 ). With increasing temperature, the relevant wavelength range shifts to the NIR and the emissive power generally increases. Further details on the calculation of the Rosseland extinction coefficient are described in [5].

For the application of Planck's law, it is assumed that the spectral radiative properties of the foam are independent of the sample temperature. Although several authors have demonstrated an at least slight effect of the sample temperature on its spectral radiative behavior [23,47–49], this effect will be neglected here in view of the insufficient quantifiability and measurement limitations for spectral radiative properties at higher temperatures [49].

For materials with anisotropic scattering, the Rosseland diffusion approximation can still be applied, using a weighted Rosseland extinction coefficient

in Eq. (6) [14,29,31]. This weighted coefficient corresponds to the extinction coefficient assuming isotropic scattering, which is why this procedure is also called isotropic scaling [31].

The investigated foams are classified into two groups according to their general radiative behavior: foams with opaque, carbon-containing strut materials and foams with partially semi-transparent strut materials made primarily from “light” oxidic ceramics. In this section, for one representative foam of each group, the measured spectral transmittance (TR) and reflectance (REFL) as well as spectral radiative properties (extinction coefficient and scattering albedo) determined by the parameter identification method using appropriate phase functions, are presented. Subsequently, the influence of several parameters, like the foam thickness, the pore size and the bulk or coating material on the spectral or temperature-dependent extinction coefficient is shown. Finally, the results are validated with thermal conductivity measurements and a radiation model.

Besides the measured spectral transmittance and reflectance of the 10 ppi SiC (I) foam, Fig. 5 shows the resulting extinction coefficients and scattering albedos applying the phase function of the diffuse reflecting spheres (DRS, Eq. (3)), which is suggested for foams with opaque strut material. Furthermore, the spectral radiative properties calculated using the isotropic phase function (ISO) are plotted, which are relevant for the calculation of Rosseland extinction coefficient in case of anisotropic scattering media. To increase the accuracy of the results, the sample thickness was reduced to s ≈ 10 mm.

The SiC (I) foam is characterized by continuously low, largely constant reflectance and transmittance, resulting in largely wavelength-independent radiative properties across the investigated wavelength range. Using the DRS phase function (g = −0.44), the scattering albedo varies between approx. 0.15 and 0.4, indicating that most of the radiation is absorbed instead of scattered. The extinction coefficients obtained with the DRS phase function are largely constant around 200 m−1. Assuming isotropic scattering leads to, on average, 10 % higher extinction coefficients, as stronger forward scattering must be based on a stronger initial radiation attenuation.

To investigate the semi-transparency of the ceramics, Fig. 6 summarizes the results of the spectroscopic measurements performed with the 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam in comparison with its base material. In addition, values of hemispherical transmittance and reflectance obtained through simulations by Li et al. [14] for an alumina foam (s = 20 mm, dp = 3.15 mm, ψ = 0.88) are plotted for reference.

The measurement curves of the dense Al2O3 (p) ceramic and the 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam are qualitatively similar, indicating that the radiation behavior of the foam is strongly dominated by its base material. In contrast to the SiC foams, alumina ceramics show a strong wavelength-dependent radiative behavior and semi-transparency with an increased transmittance and reflectance at shorter wavelengths (see Fig. 6). Bityukov and Petrov [50] define alumina as semi-transparent in a wavelength range of 0.15 μm to 6.2 μm, where the absorption coefficient is < 10 cm−1 (= 1000 m−1). If the struts are semi-transparent, radiation of shorter wavelength can partially pass through the materials without being absorbed as longer waves, but scattered instead at grain boundaries and within the strut porosity (volume scattering) [27]. This increases the measured reflectance as well as transmittance due to forward scattering.

The radiative parameters identified in this study for different ceramic base materials agree very well with the findings presented by Bityukov and Petrov [50]: For example, carbon-containing base materials have absorption coefficients of almost always > 2000 m−1 and are therefore justifiably assumed to be opaque in the whole wavelength range. For the investigated alumina base material, a sudden increase of the absorption coefficient from 300–700 m−1 to 2000–3000 m−1 was observed with an excess of the specified limit (1000 m−1) at 6.0 μm (see Table 3 ). Below this wavelength, the material can therefore be termed as semi-transparent. Other oxidic ceramic materials show a transition from semi-transparent to opaque behavior (α > 1000m−1) at similar wavelengths, for example, Al2O3 (c) at 5.6 μm, MgAl2O4 at 5.5 μm.

With increasing wavelength, the absorption coefficient of pure alumina greatly increases due to multiphonon absorption [27,51], causing a strong decrease in the measured reflectance and transmittance of the base materials and foams (see Fig. 6). Moreover, the measured reflectance, but not the transmittance, significantly increases again at λ > 10 µm because of the reststrahlen effect (lattice vibration) [27]. This effect is only pronounced in the case of Al2O3 (p), not with other semi-transparent ceramics investigated.

Although deviating geometric foam parameters permit only a qualitative analysis, the measurements with the Al2O3 (p) foam significantly deviate from the simulated results of Li et al. [14]. The experimental results show a reduced transmittance between 2 μm and 5 μm with a local minimum of the transmittance and reflectance around 3 μm, probably arising from the adsorption of water molecules and the hydroxyl bonds on the ceramic surface [27,51]. This results in an increased absorption of radiation instead of reflection and transmission around these wavelengths, which are not considered in the input data for the numerical simulation.

The correlation for the asymmetry factor g proposed by Li et al. [7] (Eq. (4)) allows the estimation of the scattering behavior in the semi-transparent range, considering both the geometric properties of the foam (see Table 2) and the spectral radiative properties of the ceramic. In Table 3, the identified absorption and scattering coefficients of the base material, as well as resulting asymmetry factors for the 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam obtained from Eq. (4) for certain wavelengths, are summarized. A significantly less pronounced transition from backward to strongly forward scattering, as presented by Li et al. [14] (see Fig. 3), can be observed. Besides the above-described increased absorption, this might be due to the smaller pore of the foam investigated by Li et al. [14], leading to a reduced optical thickness of the strut and an increased forward scattering. For the considered 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam, scattering up to λ ≈ 5 μm can be regarded as nearly isotropic (g ≈ 0). As minor deviation from g = 0 and the exact form of the phase function were demonstrated to have <5 % effect on the identified parameters, the simple ISO phase function can be assumed for the semi-transparent wavelength range of the Al2O3 (p) ceramic foam. Based on the strong increase of the absorption coefficient, the strut material can be considered as opaque at λ ≥ 6 μm.

Fig. 7 compares the identified spectral radiative properties of a 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam obtained by the parameter identification method using different relevant phase functions. While the ISO phase function is expected to represent the scattering behavior below 6 μm, the DRS phase function is assumed to be suitable for the wavelength range where struts behave as opaque.

In the semi-transparent wavelength range and for λ ≥ 10 μm, the applied scattering phase function has a considerable influence on the results, since the material exhibits strong scattering instead of absorbing behavior. Assuming higher forward scattering, the extinction coefficient, i.e., the actual attenuation of radiation, must be higher to maintain the same transmittance. In addition, the scattering albedo slightly increases, indicating that the proportion of scattered radiation increases while the absorption coefficient is nearly constant.

For the semi-transparent range (λ ≤ 5 μm) with isotropic scattering, extinction coefficients reach over 400 m−1, which decrease to approx. 250 m−1 when absorption dominates. The scattering albedo decreases from 1 to below 0.2. As revealed from the literature research, in the wavelength range where the strut material behaves as opaque, the usage of the DRS phase function is recommended. This leads to nearly constant extinction coefficients of approx. 250–300 m−1 despite the increase in reflectivity of the sample at λ > 10 μm, resulting in a scattering albedo of up to 0.9. Because of the high reflectance, isotropically weighted extinction coefficients required for the application of the Rosseland approximation are up to 40 % higher at λ > 10 μm than those calculated presuming strong backward scattering.

A parametric study has been carried out to investigate the effect of the radiative properties with respect to the sample thickness, the material and coating of the struts, as well as the pore size. While the influence of the foam thickness is studied on the basis of the spectral extinction coefficient, further investigations focus on the temperature-dependency of the Rosseland extinction coefficient (see Section 2.6). In both cases, weighted (isotropically scaled) extinction coefficients (β* or β*R) received from parameter identification are applied, allowing the comparison of materials with different (anisotropic) scattering behavior.

In a previous study [5], samples of reduced thickness were considered, because measurements with several typical industrial foams with s ≈ 20 mm showed disturbances and increased noise in the transmittance. However, reducing the sample thickness might also restrict the accuracy of the deduced radiative properties because of an insufficient representative volume element. For verification, the weighted spectral extinction coefficients β* of samples with different thicknesses (s ≈ 5–20 mm, see Table 1) from several foam types (Al2O3 (c) 10 & 30 ppi, Al2O3 -C 10 ppi and SiC (I) 10 ppi) were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 8 ((i)-(iii)). Different line styles indicate the general level of transmittance (</> 2 %).

For all foams, the highest extinction coefficients are observed at s ≈ 5 mm, which can be attributed to the lower porosity of the sample near the surface layer resulting from the manufacturing process (see Fig. 1). Furthermore, as the 10 ppi sample consists of only approx. one cell in depth, the representativeness of the measurement is limited, so that the usage of such thin samples is not recommended.

In contrast, a high sample thickness (e.g., 20 mm), leading to a transmittance < 2 % (dashed lines), can cause various interferences and inaccuracies in the spectra. These include a drop in extinction coefficient at the limits of the measurement interval due to low signal intensity and increased noise. Furthermore, low transmittance can reduce the general measurement accuracy, which may explain the high differences observed between the Al2O3 (c) 30 ppi samples of 10–20 mm in the MIR range. However, small discrepancies can also be observed in the semi-transparent wavelength range despite significantly increased transmission levels and sufficiently high signal intensity, which is likely due to the measurement accuracy and inhomogeneities of the samples.

In summary, the sample thickness should be reduced to such an extent that the transmittance is at least 2 %. However, a sufficiently large representative volume (e.g., at least two cell diameters as sample thickness) should be also maintained.

In Fig. 9 , the temperature variation of the weighted Rosseland extinction coefficients
β R *
is compared for (i) differently coated Al2O3 (p) 10 ppi foams, as well as (ii) numerous commercial 10 ppi foams made of various typical ceramic materials. Since at room temperature all foams are opaque, as radiation largely occurs at λ ≥ 6 μm (see Chap. 3.1.2), the extinction coefficient is only influenced by the structural characteristics of the foams and the surface reflectivity of the struts. The surface reflectivity generally plays a minor role for the investigated materials, as the measured reflectance of the foams is usually significantly below 0.2. An exception is the Al2O3 (p) with a strong increase in reflectance at λ > 10 μm (see Fig. 7), causing an increase in the isotropically weighted extinction coefficient at room temperature compared with the differently coated foams (see Fig. 9(i)). Also, the extinction coefficient of the MgAl2O4-coated foam is slightly higher due to the high coating thickness and the resulting reduction of porosity and pore size.

As represented in Fig. 9(ii), even though only 10 ppi open-cell ceramic foams are compared, extinction coefficients at room temperature range from approx. 200 m−1 up to 300 m−1 due to slightly differing geometric characteristics (see Table 1). The Al2O3 (c) foam exhibits the lowest extinction coefficient at room temperature due to its significantly larger pores (5.5 mm) and high porosity at the same time. In contrast, the reduced pore size (4.6 mm instead of > 5 mm) and the high reflectance of the Al2O3 (p) foam at λ > 10 µm or the reduced porosity of the ZrO2 (I) foam (76 % instead of mostly > 80 %) cause comparatively higher extinction. Furthermore, other geometric features, such as the material distribution in the foam, closed windows and strut or node thickness, are expected to influence the extinction coefficient but can hardly be quantified.

With increasing temperature, the relevant wavelength range shifts towards shorter wavelengths, including the semi-transparent wavelength range for oxidic ceramics (see Fig. 4, Fig. 5 and Fig. 7), and the extinction coefficients
β R *
for those foams increase nearly linearly. This increase varies between approx. 50 m−1 (ZrO2 (II)), 100 m−1 (Al2O3 (p), Al2O3 (c), Al2O3 (p) with TiO2 coating and ZrO2 (I)) and 200 m−1 (Al2O3 (p) with MgAl2O4 Coating) for a temperature increase from 25 °C to 700 °C, corresponding to a relative change of approx. + 20 % to + 65 % in the specified temperature range. An interpretation of the variation of the extinction coefficients and a comparison of the different materials is difficult, since the extinction coefficients at higher temperatures are mainly determined by the radiative behavior in the semi-transparent range, including both geometric conditions and radiative properties of the strut material and their interaction.

In contrast, extinction coefficients of the opaque SiC (I), SiC (II) and Al2O3-C foams, as well as Al2O3 (p) foam with Al2O3-C coating, remain nearly constant with increasing temperature, as could be expected from the wavelength-independent radiative properties (see Fig. 5). This proves that the coating is decisive for the foam’s radiative characteristics. Even the thin Al2O3-C coating of approx. ≈ 0.1 mm thickness conceals the radiation behavior of the underlying alumina foam.

Al2O3 (c) and SiC (I) foams of 10 to 30 ppi were used to investigate the influence of the pore size on the radiative properties in detail (see Fig. 10 ). At room temperature, when the oxidic foam struts are opaque, the extinction coefficients
β
R
*
of both materials match well for the respective ppi numbers, suggesting similar porosities and pore sizes of the foams (ψ ≈ 80.6–82.0 %; d p≈ 5.1–5.5 mm, 3.8 mm, 2.8–2.9 mm; see Table 1) and confirming the negligible influence of the material in case of low surface reflectivity.

In general, the extinction coefficient increases with decreasing pore size due to the increased number of struts, the higher surface area and increased strut curvature, which enhance the scattering and absorption possibilities [8]. The relation can be described by a power-law function, as also shown by Heisig et al. [5], comparing different models for the prediction of the extinction coefficient. As discussed before, the extinction of the SiC (I) foams remains nearly constant with increasing temperature, whereas Al2O3 (c) foams show a similar, nearly linear increase of the extinction coefficients for all pore sizes due to their semi-transparency in the range of lower wavelength.

Fig. 11 shows a comparison of the measured and modelled effective thermal conductivities (ETC) of a 10 ppi Al2O3 (p) foam at temperatures up to 700 °C, allowing the validation of the applied modelling and the assessment of the plausibility of the identified radiative properties. The superposition of the decreasing thermal conductivity of the solid and the increasing radiative component, modelled by the Rosseland diffusion approximation using isotropically scaled extinction coefficients, leads to a temperature variation typical for highly porous media with a local minimum of the ETC, which is observed at around 300 °C to 400 °C for the Al2O3 (p) foam. As shown in Fig. 11, the measured ETC could be predicted by the model with an accuracy of approx. 10 % (error bars) over the investigated temperature range of 20 °C to 700 °C, suggesting that the results of the model-based parameter identification reproduce very well the actual radiative heat transfer in the foam structure.

Similarly, Fig. 12 presents the thermal conductivities of the differently coated Al2O3 (p) foams. The conductive heat transfer differs only slightly from that of the original Al2O3 (p) foam, except for the MgAl2O4-coated foam, because of its relatively high coating thickness. While the radiative behavior of the TiO2 coating is largely equivalent to that of Al2O3 (p), resulting in similar ETCs, the MgAl2O4 coating slightly impaired radiation so that even at 700 °C, the conduction still dominates. For both coatings, the model can accurately reproduce the variation of the ETC with increasing temperature, with an uncertainty of approx. ± 10 %.

For the Al2O3-C-coated foam, the model constantly overestimates the measured ETC by up to approx. 15 % at temperatures above 200 °C. A reason could be the inaccurate measurement results caused by a high sample thickness and low transmittance, as no reduction of the thickness was possible with these samples. Nevertheless, the variation of thermal conductivity with increasing temperature is also well reproduced for this coating. Further inaccuracies of the model predictions might result from the limited accuracy of the determined porosity and assumed material properties, as well as the inhomogeneities in the foam structure, such as their porosity profile.

In summary, the presented modelling allows an estimation of the ETC with reasonably high accuracy, as shown by the agreement with the thermal conductivity measurements for several different foams. This supports the plausibility of the identified radiative properties from the parameter identification method. Furthermore, the results prove the applicability of the Rosseland diffusion approximation for a rough estimate of the radiative heat transfer at temperatures up to 700 °C, even for partially semi-transparent foams with an optical thickness of τ ≈ 8…10, as long as anisotropic scattering is considered.

In this study, the radiative properties of a variety of ceramic open-cell foams with different scattering behavior were determined, combining spectroscopic measurements with a numerical approach, namely a parameter identification method, based on the solution of the RTE. The influence of various foam parameters was analyzed. For validation, measurements of the ETC were compared with the modelling results using the Rosseland approximation. The main findings are:
•
The radiative behavior of the foams is clearly determined by the ceramic material. Significant differences in radiative behavior were observed depending on whether the ceramic strut material behaves as opaque or semi-transparent in the relevant wavelength range.
•
At room temperature, the weighted Rosseland extinction coefficient is influenced by both the surface reflectivity of the material and the geometric characteristics of the foam, such as pore size and porosity.
•
For foams with partially semi-transparent strut material, the attenuation of the radiation increases nearly linearly with increasing temperature, with the slope depending on both the geometric features of the foam and the radiative properties of the strut material, as well as their interaction.
•
The coating of the foam, even if its thickness is below 100 µm, strongly determines the radiative behavior, which enables a targeted functionalization and adaptation of the heat transfer properties according to the intended application.
•
The good agreement between the modelled ETC, based on the identified radiative parameters, and the ETC, measured by the Hot Disk Method (mainly ± 10 %), demonstrates the reasonability of the identified radiative properties and suggests the suitability of the applied methods and models. However, recommendations about the sample thickness and the different, partly anisotropic scattering of the foams need to be considered.

In conclusion, the current study demonstrates a method for a reliable determination of the parameters, which are essential for the prediction of radiative heat transfer in ceramic open-cell foams. It can be utilized to extend and systemize the existing database of thermal properties of ceramic open-cell foams. The presented results can be useful for the validation of exclusively numerical studies. Moreover, based on the great amount of data gained in this study, future work could focus on the improvement of empirical correlations, enabling a fast and simple estimation of the extinction coefficient to predict radiative heat transfer in various applications.

Finally, for the investigated materials, the temperature dependence of the radiation properties is well represented by the wavelength dependence described by Planck’s law. However, the effect of the actual sample temperature on its radiative behavior, especially for semi-transparent ceramics, demonstrated by several authors [23,47–49], might require further investigations in the future.
Data will be made available on request.
